title: Staying cool through summer
excerpt: In peak summer your body's strength dips and the digestive fire weakens. Ayurveda's grishma ritucharya is a simple seasonal reset: cool, light food, more fluids, and a gentler daily rhythm.
body_html:
why summer feels draining

By April and May most of us notice the same things: less appetite, more thirst, a heavy, tired feeling by afternoon, and skin that flushes and sweats easily. This is not weakness, it is the season acting on the body exactly as the old texts describe.

Ayurveda calls the hot, dry part of the year grishma ritu (the summer season). The classical view is that the sun's sharp rays steadily draw moisture and strength out of living things, so a person's bala (strength or stamina) is at its lowest in summer (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3, Ritucharya). At the same time the heat aggravates pitta, the fire-and-water dosha that governs digestion and body heat (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 6). Because the body's own heat is spread thin to the surface for sweating, the agni, the digestive fire, runs weaker in this season. That single fact, a low digestive fire in a pitta-heavy season, explains most of what summer asks of us: eat lighter, cool down, and do not overload the system.

a low digestive fire in a pitta-heavy season

what to eat and drink in the heat

The granthas are unusually practical about summer food. They recommend tastes and qualities that are sweet, light, cooling, and a little oily or unctuous, and they advise stepping back from food that is too salty, too sour, too pungent, or too hot (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3). In everyday terms that means more of what is naturally cooling: rice, milk, ghee in small amounts, ripe sweet fruit, cucumber, and bottle gourd, and less of the deep-fried, heavily spiced, fermented, and sour-salty food that feels heavy in the heat.

Fluids matter more now than in any other season. Classical summer favourites include mantha, a light cooling drink of roasted grain flour stirred into water, and plain or lightly sweetened water, taken through the day rather than gulped ice-cold all at once (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 6). Sattu drinks, thin buttermilk, coconut water, and fruit like watermelon and grapes fit this same spirit. The point is steady, gentle hydration that replaces what sweat takes away, not so much ice that it shocks the already-weak digestive fire.

Alcohol gets a specific caution. The texts warn against much wine or strong drink in summer, since it adds heat and dryness to an already hot, dry season (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3). If taken at all, it should be very dilute.

a lighter daily rhythm

Summer is the one season where Ayurveda openly relaxes its usual rules. The midday sun is to be avoided: stay in shade and cool places during the fiercest hours, and let the evening and night, when a breeze and the moon cool the air, be your active and restful time (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3).

A short daytime rest is actually permitted in summer, even though sleeping by day is discouraged in most other seasons, precisely because the nights are short and the days are exhausting (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 6). Keep clothing light and loose, favour cotton, and use simple cooling touches the texts mention in spirit: a cool bath, sandalwood paste on the skin, gardens, water, and the company of cool, calm surroundings. Heavy exercise and anything that overheats the body, including too much sun, sex, and physical strain, are best dialled down now and saved for cooler months.

when to see a doctor

Most summer tiredness, thirst, and reduced appetite settle with cooling food, fluids, rest, and shade. But heat can turn dangerous. See a doctor straight away for signs of heat illness: a very high body temperature, hot dry skin with little or no sweating, confusion or fainting, a racing heart, severe headache, or vomiting that stops you keeping fluids down. Children, older people, pregnant women, and anyone with heart, kidney, or blood-pressure conditions need extra care and earlier help.

Also see a qualified vaidya or doctor if low appetite, loose motions, burning urination, dizziness, or skin rashes are severe, keep returning, or last beyond a few days. This article is general seasonal guidance from the Ayurvedic tradition, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care.

Try This Today

  • Sip, don't gulp: Keep a bottle of plain or lightly sweetened water near you and drink small amounts through the day. Steady hydration replaces what sweat takes better than one icy glass that shocks a weak summer digestion.
  • Make the afternoon quiet: Plan errands and any exercise for early morning or evening and keep the fierce midday hours for shade, rest, or a short nap, which the texts specifically allow in summer.
  • Cool the plate: Lean on rice, thin buttermilk, cucumber, bottle gourd, and ripe sweet fruit like watermelon. Pull back on deep-fried, very sour, salty, and heavily spiced dishes that feel heavy in the heat.
  • Dress and bathe for the season: Wear loose cotton, take a cool (not freezing) bath, and use simple cooling touches like sandalwood paste, a breezy room, or time near water in the evening.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: In peak summer your body's strength dips and the digestive fire weakens. Ayurveda's grishma ritucharya is a simple seasonal reset: cool, light food, more fluids, and a gentler daily rhythm.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Summer (grishma ritu) lowers your strength and weakens digestion, so the season calls for lighter, cooling food.
Favour sweet, cool, light foods and steady fluids like buttermilk, sattu, coconut water, and fruit; ease off sour, salty, very spicy, fried food and alcohol.
Avoid the midday sun, rest in the shade, keep activity gentle, and a short daytime nap is allowed in summer.

glossary_terms.definition: The hot, dry summer season in Ayurveda's calendar.
The seasonal regimen: adjusting food and routine to each season.
The fire-and-water dosha governing digestion and body heat; it rises in summer.
The digestive fire; it runs weaker in the summer heat.
Bodily strength or stamina, which the texts say is lowest in summer.
A light cooling drink of roasted grain flour stirred into water.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 6 (Tasyashitiya, seasonal regimen)
Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana (Ritucharya, the six seasons)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "grishma ritu" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, grishma ritu is defined as: The hot, dry summer season in Ayurveda's calendar.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to sip, don't gulp. Keep a bottle of plain or lightly sweetened water near you and drink small amounts through the day. Steady hydration replaces what sweat takes better than one icy glass that shocks a weak summer digestion.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to make the afternoon quiet. Plan errands and any exercise for early morning or evening and keep the fierce midday hours for shade, rest, or a short nap, which the texts specifically allow in summer.
